county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1838 from Penobscot and Somerset counties. Maine's least populous county. Named for the Piscataquis River. Dover-Foxcroft is the county seat. Contains Moosehead Lake region and the northern approach to Mount Katahdin (Baxter State Park).
county_metadata.research_tips: Piscataquis County is Maine's most rural and sparsely settled county, which affects record availability — some unorganized townships never had town clerks, and vital events were recorded in adjacent organized towns or at the county level. For pre-1838 records, check Penobscot County (Bangor) for the eastern portions and Somerset County (Skowhegan) for the western areas. The Registry of Deeds in Dover-Foxcroft has records from 1838. Logging and sporting camp records are unique sources — Great Northern Paper Company records and the Moosehead Lake region's sporting lodge registers document seasonal populations. The Thompson Free Library in Dover-Foxcroft holds local history materials. Katahdin Iron Works records (1843-1890) document a short-lived iron-smelting community. The Piscataquis County Historical Society preserves local family files. Monson slate quarry records document Welsh and Finnish immigrant stonecutters.

source_profiles.repository: Piscataquis County Register of Deeds, Dover-Foxcroft
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from 1838. Pre-1838 in Penobscot and Somerset counties. Extensive timber land records.
Wills, administrations from 1838. Sparse population means fewer probate files than other counties.

source_profiles.source_name: Maine Vital Records — Piscataquis County Towns
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Town-level vital records. Some gaps for unorganized townships where no town clerk existed.
Federal population schedules 1840-1950. Unorganized townships enumerated under plantation or county designations.
Rural cemetery transcriptions. Many remote burying grounds in former logging communities.
Congregational and Baptist church records from Dover, Foxcroft, Guilford, Monson, and other towns.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Variable — some deposited at Maine State Archives.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1854 from Lincoln County. Maine's smallest county by area. Named for an early name for the lower Kennebec River region. Bath is the county seat, famous as a shipbuilding center (Bath Iron Works). Popham Colony (1607) was the first English settlement attempt in Maine.
county_metadata.research_tips: Sagadahoc County is Maine's smallest but has outsized genealogical importance due to Bath's shipbuilding industry. Bath Iron Works (founded 1884) and its predecessors employed thousands — shipyard employment records, union documents, and the Maine Maritime Museum in Bath are key sources. For pre-1854 records, check Lincoln County in Wiscasset. The Sagadahoc County Registry of Deeds in Bath has records from 1854. The Patten Free Library in Bath has an excellent local history room with ship registers, crew lists, and Bath family files. Maritime records at NARA Waltham include vessel registrations, customs house records, and seamen's protection certificates from the Bath customs district. The Maine Maritime Museum holds the largest collection of shipbuilding records in New England. Town records for Bath, Woolwich, Georgetown, and Phippsburg (Popham Colony site) are rich colonial-era sources available through FamilySearch.

source_profiles.repository: Sagadahoc County Register of Deeds, Bath
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from 1854. Pre-1854 in Lincoln County.
Wills, administrations from 1854. Shipbuilder estates often highly detailed.

source_profiles.source_name: Maine Vital Records — Sagadahoc County Towns
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Town-level vital records for Bath, Woolwich, Georgetown, Phippsburg, Topsham, and others.
Federal population schedules 1860-1950. Earlier censuses under Lincoln County.
Bath newspapers covering shipbuilding industry, maritime news, and local events.

source_profiles.source_name: Civil War Naval Service Records — Bath
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Bath men served in both Army and Navy — naval records particularly relevant given the maritime economy.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1809 from Kennebec County. Large rural county in central Maine extending to the Canadian border. Named for Somerset, England. Skowhegan is the county seat. Logging and farming economy. Bingham was named for William Bingham, the land speculator.
county_metadata.research_tips: Somerset County stretches from the Kennebec River valley to the Quebec border and has distinct research zones. For pre-1809 records check Kennebec County (Augusta) and ultimately Lincoln County (Wiscasset). The southern towns (Skowhegan, Norridgewock, Madison) have records from the late 1700s, while the northern unorganized territories (Jackman, Moose River) were settled much later and have sparser records. The Bingham Purchase (William Bingham acquired 2 million acres in 1793) shaped settlement patterns — land records relating to this massive grant are at Maine State Archives. The Skowhegan History House and Skowhegan Public Library hold local family files. The Arnold Expedition route (1775 march to Quebec) passed through Somerset County — some markers and records survive. Jackman and the northern border area have French Canadian connections with Quebec's Beauce region. Somerset County Registry of Deeds in Skowhegan has records from 1809.

source_profiles.repository: Somerset County Register of Deeds, Skowhegan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from 1809. Pre-1809 in Kennebec County. Bingham Purchase records at Maine State Archives.
Wills, administrations from 1809.

source_profiles.source_name: Maine Vital Records — Somerset County Towns
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Town-level vital records for Skowhegan, Norridgewock, Madison, Anson, and other towns.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good for southern towns; sparser for northern areas.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal population schedules 1810-1950. Northern plantations and unorganized townships enumerated separately.
Cemetery transcriptions for Skowhegan, Norridgewock, and rural Somerset County.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good for settled areas; gaps in remote north.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Property tax records for tracking land ownership and family wealth.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1827 from parts of Hancock, Kennebec, and Lincoln counties. Named for General Samuel Waldo, colonial-era land proprietor. Belfast is the county seat. Mid-coast Maine county. Searsport was once the second largest deepwater port in Maine.
county_metadata.research_tips: Waldo County sits on mid-coast Maine and its records split among three parent counties before 1827. For pre-1827 land records, determine which parent county held jurisdiction: Hancock County (Ellsworth) for the eastern towns, Lincoln County (Wiscasset) for the southern towns, and Kennebec County (Augusta) for the western towns. The Waldo Patent (Samuel Waldo's 18th-century land grant) predates all these counties — related records are at Maine State Archives and Massachusetts Historical Society. Belfast Free Library has a strong local history collection. Searsport's Penobscot Marine Museum holds outstanding maritime records — crew lists, shipping logs, and captain's papers — essential for tracing seafaring families. The Waldo County Registry of Deeds in Belfast has records from 1827. Belfast was an important shipbuilding and chicken-processing center. The Belfast Historical Society and Liberty Historical Society preserve family papers. Islesboro island records require special attention as access was limited.

source_profiles.repository: Waldo County Register of Deeds, Belfast
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from 1827. Pre-1827 split among Hancock, Kennebec, and Lincoln counties.
Wills, administrations from 1827. Earlier probate in parent counties.

source_profiles.source_name: Maine Vital Records — Waldo County Towns
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Town-level vital records for Belfast, Searsport, Winterport, Islesboro, and other towns.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — New England town clerk tradition.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal population schedules 1830-1950. County formed 1827, first full census 1830.
Congregational, Baptist, and Quaker records from Belfast, Montville, and other towns. Quaker meetings in Montville area notable.
Primary Waldo County newspaper. Maritime news, local events, obituaries, legal notices.
State death registrations from 1892. Earlier deaths in town clerk records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1789 from Lincoln County while Maine was part of Massachusetts. Easternmost county in the U.S. Named for George Washington. Machias is the county seat. Passamaquoddy Bay and Quoddy Head. Major blueberry and sardine industries. Eastport is the easternmost city in the U.S.
county_metadata.research_tips: Washington County is Maine's Downeast frontier — the easternmost county in the United States. For pre-1789 records check Lincoln County (Wiscasset). The Registry of Deeds in Machias has records from 1789. The Passamaquoddy tribe (Pleasant Point and Indian Township reservations) has tribal enrollment and genealogical records distinct from county records — BIA records at NARA Waltham are important. Maritime records for Eastport, Lubec, and Machias ports are at NARA Waltham (customs records, vessel registrations). The Machiasport 'Battle of the Margaretta' (1775, first naval engagement of the Revolution) is documented in local archives. Washington County has strong economic ties to New Brunswick, Canada — cross-border families are common and Canadian records should be checked. The Burnham Tavern Museum in Machias, Machias Bay Area Historical Society, and Eastport Area Historical Society hold family files. Sardine canning factory records (Eastport, Lubec) document immigrant labor from Maritime Canada and Scandinavia.

source_profiles.repository: Washington County Register of Deeds, Machias
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from 1789. Pre-1789 in Lincoln County.
Wills, administrations from 1789. Early estate inventories reflect frontier conditions.

source_profiles.source_name: Maine Vital Records — Washington County Towns
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Town-level vital records for Machias, Eastport, Calais, Lubec, Cherryfield, and other towns.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — some gaps in remote plantation records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal population schedules 1790-1950. Pre-1820 enumerated as Massachusetts.

source_profiles.source_name: Canadian Border Crossing Records — Washington County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Border crossing records for Calais, Eastport, and other Washington County ports of entry. Critical for cross-border families.
Cemetery transcriptions including early coastal burying grounds and remote inland cemeteries.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Fair — ongoing efforts in remote areas.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Regional Downeast newspapers. Shipping news, lumbering activity, legal notices.

county_metadata.record_losses: No significant county-level record losses. York County has some of the oldest surviving court records in New England.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Oldest county in Maine, established 1636 as Yorkshire under the Province of Maine, then absorbed into Massachusetts 1652. Pre-dates Maine statehood by 184 years. Alfred is the county seat (since 1808). Contains Kittery (oldest town in Maine), Kennebunk, Biddeford, Saco, Sanford, and Old Orchard Beach. Biddeford-Saco textile mills attracted French Canadian workers.
county_metadata.research_tips: York County is Maine's oldest county and has records stretching back to the 1630s — among the oldest English court records in North America. The York County Registry of Deeds in Alfred has volumes from 1642, making it one of the oldest continuous land record series in the country. The Province and Court Records of Maine (published by Maine Historical Society) transcribe early York County court proceedings. For pre-1760 genealogy anywhere in Maine, York County records may be relevant as it originally encompassed all of the Province of Maine. The Old York Historical Society and Brick Store Museum (Kennebunk) hold outstanding family papers and town records. Biddeford-Saco was a major textile center with large Franco-American communities — St. Joseph's and St. Andre's parish records in Biddeford are critical French-language sources. The McArthur Public Library in Biddeford has local history materials. Kittery's naval yard (Portsmouth Navy Yard) records are at NARA. Alfred's courthouse records include original 17th-century documents.
county_metadata.heritage_book: Province and Court Records of Maine, Vols. I-VI (Maine Historical Society)
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Register of Deeds online portal — among the oldest deed records in the U.S.

source_profiles.repository: York County Registry of Deeds, Alfred
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from 1642 — among the oldest continuous deed series in North America. Grantor/grantee indexes.
Probate records from 1687. Among the oldest probate files in Maine. Original 17th-century wills and inventories.

source_profiles.source_name: Maine Vital Records — York County Towns
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Town-level vital records from earliest settlement. Kittery records from 1630s. Published vital records series.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Very good — some of New England's oldest town records.

source_profiles.repository: Maine Historical Society / Maine State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Province and Court Records of Maine. Colonial-era court proceedings, depositions, and legal documents published in six volumes.
Federal population schedules 1790-1950. Pre-1820 enumerated as Massachusetts.

source_profiles.source_name: York County Church Records — Catholic and Protestant
source_profiles.repository: Diocese of Portland / Maine Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Congregational records from colonial era. Catholic parish records from Biddeford-Saco Franco-American parishes (French-language registers).

source_profiles.source_name: Biddeford-Saco Journal / York County Coast Star
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Regional newspapers covering Biddeford-Saco-Kennebunk area. French-language papers like Le Messager also published.
Naturalization records from federal and state courts. Irish and French Canadian immigrants prominent.
